Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only variable assignments and function definitions in its global scope. No command substitutions, backtick executions, or other top-level code that would run during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The `source` array points to the official upstream GitHub releases and a license file, both with pinned SHA256 checksums (not SKIP). No suspicious URLs, obfuscation, or unexpected network destinations. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions are present but are not executed by the requested commands, so they are out of scope for this narrow safety gate. Fetching the sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>Safe: no risky top-level code or sources</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard packaging practices for an AppImage. It downloads both the AppImage and the LICENSE file from the official upstream GitHub repository (`satisfactorymodding/SatisfactoryModManager`). Checksums (`sha256sums`) are provided and not set to `SKIP`, ensuring integrity. The `prepare()` function extracts the AppImage to adjust the `.desktop` file's `Exec` line—a routine modification to make the desktop entry work outside the AppImage container. The `package()` function installs the AppImage, desktop file, icons, license, and creates a symlink. No dangerous commands, obfuscated code, unexpected network requests, or exfiltration attempts are present. The only network activity is fetching the package's own upstream assets.
</details>
<summary>Standard AppImage PKGBUILD with no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

It contains only legal text and no executable code, obfuscated commands, network requests, or system modifications. There are no indicators of malicious behavior. The presence of a GPL-3.0 license file in an AUR package is normal and expected.
</details>
<summary>Standard GPL license file, no security concerns.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/satisfactory-mod-manager-appimage.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-_a8n_8uf...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-_a8n_8uf
Analyzing satisfactory-mod-manager-appimage AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-_a8n_8uf) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only sources content from trusted GitHub domains (github.com and raw.githubusercontent.com) under the `satisfactorymodding/SatisfactoryModManager` repository, which is consistent with the package's stated upstream. The source URLs use the tagged version v3.1.0, and the AppImage and LICENSE files are directly fetched with fixed, verifiable SHA-256 checksums. There is no command substitution, dynamic fetching via curl/wget in the global scope, or execution of code during sourcing.

The `pkgver` is hardcoded as 3.1.0 and does not involve any external calls (like git describe or online queries), so no network activity occurs when sourcing the PKGBUILD. Top-level code consists only of standard variable assignments. The potentially executable operations (like `chmod +x` and AppImage extraction) occur inside the `prepare()` and `package()` functions, which are not executed under `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. Thus, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` involves only safe, declarative operations.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous operations during source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous operations during source fetch and parse[/]

LLM auditresponse for LICENSE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is the standard text of the GNU General Public License Version 3 (GPLv3), an open-source license published by the Free Software Foundation. It contains no executable code, scripts, or malicious content. The document outlines users' rights and obligations regarding software distribution, modification, and warranty disclaimers. There is no evidence of tampering, obfuscation, or supply-chain attacks. The presence of this file in a package is normal and expected when distributing software under GPLv3.
</details>
<summary>
Standard GPLv3 license text, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed LICENSE. Status: SAFE -- Standard GPLv3 license text, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a pre-built AppImage from the official GitHub repository of the Satisfactory Mod Manager and verifies its integrity using a hardcoded SHA-256 checksum. The source URL points directly to a release asset matching the declared pkgver, which is a standard and expected practice for AUR packages distributing binaries.

The prepare() function extracts the AppImage using its built-in --appimage-extract functionality, a common and safe operation. It modifies the .desktop file to disable desktop integration when run outside the AppImage context by setting DESKTOPINTEGRATION=false, which is a harmless environment variable adjustment. There is no evidence of code injection, obfuscation, or unauthorized network activity.

All installed files are either the original AppImage, the extracted contents, or symlinks to them. Icons, desktop files, and licenses are placed in standard system directories following Freedesktop conventions. No dangerous commands (e.g., curl|bash, eval, base64 decoding) are used. The package conflicts with alternative versions of the same software, which is correct hygiene. The checksums are provided and match the sources, and no commands execute unverified remote code. While distributing pre-built binaries carries inherent trust assumptions, the structure and behavior of this PKGBUILD are consistent with safe packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
Valid source, safe extraction, no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>
